story:
Once upon a time—and a place (they go together, after all)—the world was truly alive, all talking, all in color. But not Gray Goose Country. There, everything sagged into the same old shade—grass, clouds, moods—except for one spunky lap-sized ragdoll who had named herself McGee (from a cracker box, since no one—not even her ragdoll mother—had ever given her a proper name). McGee, with raggedy red yarn curls, a black thread smile, and one missing arm (a Labrador’s fault in a previous life), lived in the back of a thrift shop called Threads, tangled with two scruffy stuffed bears and a wobbly-legged kewpie doll in a wicker bin labeled “Specials” (it should have read “Discards”).

What made McGee truly special wasn’t newness; it was grit. Her mother, Mimi—beautiful even in secondhand velveteen with slightly ripped puffy sleeves—had been snatched from the bin by one of the unseen minions of Izzard the Wizard, a lazy, small-spirited show-off up in Gray Goose Castle who craved color but couldn’t conjure competence. His spells went sideways: lakes became sinkholes, meadows became frog swamps. Bored and mean, he set petty traps, bullied servants, and grabbed what wasn’t his—like Mimi—because it made him feel bigger.

McGee had a deadline: Thursday. That was the day Izzard dragged his Big Book of Wizardly Tricks and Spells out to the castle yard to practice (and fail) in public. McGee also had a mount: Big Black Dog, a mastiff the size of a miniature pony who belonged to Mrs. Trimble (fussbudgety, with gray hair and grayer moods). After hours, McGee would wrap her good arm around his neck and ride like a knight escaping a bin instead of a tower. Yes, Mrs. Trimble might toss her into the “Free” basket if caught. Yes, McGee was at the end of her ragdoll rope—pinched, yanked, redressed, and laughed at by children who preferred “new.” But the clock was ticking.

For weeks, hiding behind a cold stone fence on the gray hillside, McGee had watched Izzard bluster through spells, his big book flopped open in large print he still misread. Big Black Dog ferried her there each time, dark and silent as a shadow, and waited with her, chin on paws. She memorized the words that mattered. On Thursday, there was Mimi—set on a blanket with the Gray Goose seal, placed like a decoration just steps from Izzard and his wand. McGee fixed her eyes (that was part of the spell), whispered the words she’d learned—not the one he was flailing at (TURNING WHAT IS NEXT TO YOU INTO WHATEVER YOU WANT), but the one she needed. Presto, change!

Izzard vanished in a puff, and somewhere above the circling gray geese a new bird joined the flock, honking, flustered, temporary. The book, knocked askew, now lay open to SPELL FOR TURNING A WAYWARD WIZARD INTO A FLYING GOOSE … FOR A DAY, OR TWO. McGee ran from the fence to the book, tugged loose a single strand from her red yarn curls, flipped the heavy pages to page 4, and laid the bright thread across it. McGee then darted to Mimi, hugged her tight, and whistled for Big Black Dog, who came bounding down from the hillside; together they climbed onto his broad back and thundered down the slope.

“You’ll be back soon, Mr. Wizard,” McGee called into the gray sky. “Maybe kinder. Maybe brighter. Maybe Gray Goose will be, too.” When Izzard returned to earth—and himself—he found his Big Book waiting, open to page 4: SPELL FOR TURNING A GRAY GLOOMY KINGDOM INTO A HAPPY COLORFUL PLACE. Only three easy steps. The page was marked with a single strand of red yarn.

And that, in a land that had forgotten that color talks and hearts listen, was how a one-armed ragdoll met a deadline, rescued her mother, and nudged even a foolish wizard toward better magic.
